chapters.title: We all need to open our eyes to the many underhanded uses of bullshit
chapters.text:
You may think you live in a world of truth. But if you dig deeper, you'll see that many of the things you assume to be accurate are actually nothing short of an illusion. For sure, that can leave you feeling a little nervous. After all, how are you supposed to identify the truth from the lies? But, by learning how to call out bullshit, you'll feel far more assured in the reality of the world we live in.

Carl T. Bergstrom & Jevin D. West see bullshit everywhere. But, they've identified the reason why and where it comes from. All of this helps them decipher the truth from the lies, and they want to share their advice for you to do the same thing.

They see the calling out of bullshit as a priority because it can affect democracy. Fake news can easily cause people to sway their vote or think a certain way about a political candidate. Understanding bullshit helps you to separate all of this.

Misinformation can be a dangerous form of manipulation that flies under the radar unless you know how to spot it.

You might think that the world of bullshit we live in is something pretty new, but it's been around since the dawn of time. People have always manipulated others into thinking a certain way or doing a particular thing. The only difference now is that we have the Internet to help spread misinformation much faster and far wider.

Bullshit also comes in many forms. It can be using fancy language to describe something to the point where it sounds far better than it is; “garbage disposal unit” is one good example; it actually means trash can. Dressing things up in statistics and graphs is another. Salacious headlines and clickbait advertising takes it to another level.

The truth is that you can't always rely upon the information around you to be genuine. In this summary, you will learn how data can be skewed to look a certain way, the drivers behind it, and how you can protect yourself against bullshit. Your ability to spot misinformation relies on it.

chapters.title: Paltering is designed to mislead without being called a liar
chapters.text:
Lying is something we all know we shouldn't do, but many people do it anyway. However, you can be called out when you lie, which leads to losing friends, credibility, and people simply not trusting you. Being caught in a lie in the marketing world, politics, or any other industry that requires you to reach out to others, can be catastrophic.

Instead, people use something called paltering.

Paltering is when you lead someone to form an incorrect conclusion about the information you are relaying by saying things that are technically true but in a way that is totally out of context, leading them to the wrong conclusion on purpose. Then, if they question you on it, you have a certain amount of deniability at your leisure.

Paltering is a form of deception used to make people think a certain way or make themselves sound far more interesting than they are.

When understanding the role of bullshit in the modern world, we also need to think carefully about how one piece of incorrect information can be spread by using paltering. This can be to the point where people consider it trustworthy, even if there's no evidence to back it up. Bergstrom & West mention Brandolini's Principle.

Software engineer, Alberto Brandolini, came up with the idea that the effort required to disprove bullshit is far more significant than the effort needed to produce the bullshit in the first place. Bergstrom & West cite the false idea that childhood vaccines can cause autism as a good example here. Even though there is no evidence to prove this to be the case and plenty of evidence to prove that it isn't true, people still question it, and many believe it.

In this case, Brandolini's idea was proven correct. A small concept has taken countless years to disprove and just a few minutes to put out there for people to believe.

Did you know? Fake news is a huge problem. According to Statista, around 80% of US consumers saw fake news online about the COVID-19 pandemic.

chapters.title: The Internet and social media cause a whole lot of bullshit
chapters.text:
The overwhelming majority of people have a smartphone. You're probably looking at your smartphone right now to read or listen to this summary. So, it begs the question of why people don't just quickly fact-check things on their phone if they're not sure about something. The truth? People believe most of what they read, and they then use their phones to spread that misinformation without realizing it.

The Internet has brought us countless opportunities and benefits. But it’s also created a significant problem in spreading fake news and misinformation. It's a whole cyber world of bullshit!

Anyone can come up with a story and spread it online; they don't need credentials or proof; they can simply dress the story up in a way that looks convincing. That story may then go viral and reach a vast worldwide audience.

We can access more information than ever, but that information is less reliable. ~ Carl T. Bergstrom & Jevin D. West

We can read anything we want online, but most people rarely get to the end of a story. Instead, they click on the most salacious, attention-grabbing headline.

Algorithms are another major problem because they direct you to similar things you've looked at before, creating a confirmation bias in your mind. You start to develop a view about something based on several similar articles you've probably read. But, you don't bother to fact-check them; you blindly believe.

Then we have social media; one idea can be spread around the world with just a click. Misinformation spread via social media is a significant problem that needs tackling. False claims can look exceptionally believable, and the more 'likes' and 'shares' they get, the more you believe them to be true.

Misinformation can be as simple as dating gossip or something politically serious. Either way, it can force people to develop incorrect opinions and act in ways that could be dangerous.

One of the main reasons for misinformation may be that the first news outlet or site to break a news story will always receive more clicks and, therefore, more traffic. Everyone wants to be first, and fact-checking slows that process down. So, they don't bother to fact-check; they just publish quickly, causing the mass spread of misinformation.

chapters.title: Sometimes, numbers and statistics can tie your mind up in knots
chapters.text:
Every single thing comes down to data. These days, everything is counted and quantified. We analyze everything within an inch of its life and develop statistics and numbers to create evidence of an idea. Even websites use cookies and algorithms to track what you look at.

Data can be helpful in many ways, but it can also set us up for failure. When numbers aren't presented correctly, they can be used to deceive.

We automatically feel reassured of its credibility when we see data because we trust numbers. Many people use the phrase “show me the numbers” or “show me the data” before creating an opinion about something. We're all confident about the safety and accuracy of statistics. But, if those numbers can be manipulated to look slightly different, they can be used to create an alternate reality instead.

Words are clearly constructs of human minds, but numbers? Numbers seem to come directly from nature herself. ~ Carl T. Bergstrom & Jevin D. West

It's not that difficult to show numbers in the wrong context, twisting their meaning to offer something entirely different. It's also possible for statistics to be false because people tend to over or underestimate, creating inaccurate figures. Or a small sample of respondents used in a study may not give an accurate picture of a larger group of people. We're so focused on the “fact” that data never lies to us that we fail to see just how easy it can be wrong.

If you're collecting statistics from a group of people based on performance, their behaviors will likely change during the study because they want to prove themselves. If you offer a bonus, behavioral changes are even more likely to occur. Again, the statistics will be inaccurate.

There are many reasons why numbers and statistics can lie, but we're so fixated on the idea that we can trust them that we fail to see the bullshit.

chapters.title: If you think you can trust graphs and pie charts, you're wrong
chapters.text:
The human mind isn't designed to look at a large amount of data and understand it well; that’s something that we rely on computers to do for us. So, we input data and ask the computer to present it in a way that we can understand, usually in visual form, like a graph, pie chart, or some other chart that summarizes what the numbers mean.

It's easy to assume that visualizing data in this way can't be manipulated; after all, the computer is just displaying the numbers. But how the chart or table appears depends very heavily upon the person who tells the computer what to do. So, if a designer wants to present the information slightly differently, therefore slightly changing the meaning, we won't notice the difference. Bergstrom & West argue that our educational systems don't teach us how to read these types of charts correctly, and we simply believe whatever is in front of us. The designer of a chart or table has a tremendous influence on the message received by those viewing that material. It can be presented in a way that makes you perceive something completely different from the real meaning. For example, if you see a pie chart, your eyes will automatically be drawn to the largest section, assuming the largest section denotes the most popular choice in a survey. But, a designer can manipulate that section to be the choice that most people voted against instead. It all comes down to context.

Humans are prone to jumping to conclusions, so when a chart shows something “clearly,” we're likely to believe it.

It's essential to be more aware of this fact and put your feelings aside when viewing graphics such as this. Examine it and question whether you could read it in another way.

chapters.title: We need to learn how to be more skeptical and less accepting of what we see & hear
chapters.text:
In standard terms, being skeptical isn't necessarily a great personality trait. It can cause you to distrust everyone around you and even be a little paranoid. However, to avoid being manipulated by data and fake news, you need to learn how to be a little skeptical when the situation calls for it.

There are many ways you can sidestep the chances of being taken in by bullshit you see online and in the real world. Bergstrom & West have a few simple suggestions on how you can do just that.
• Question where the information has come from and ask who has written it, how they know about it, and what they're trying to sell.
• Be mindful of any comparisons between statistics that seem unfair or unrealistic, like comparing a small country to a large one.
• Remember that if something seems too good or even too bad to be true, the chances are that it is.
• Always remember that anything can be written to mean one or several things. Dig down to the source of the information and question it further.
• We believe what we already know, and we look for confirmation that we're right. So, be aware of confirmation bias by understanding your current views. Be brave enough to seek other views and opinions rather than falling foul of misinformation caused by confirmation bias.
• There are always several hypotheses to think about at any one time; one person's theory isn't necessarily true. Bergstrom & West give the example of Disney's stock value falling by 2.5% shortly after the Roseanne Barr show was canceled due to a racist tweet. For sure, the drop could have been down to that, but it could have been caused by several other things too. So, never assume.

If you're feeling overwhelmed by the amount of bullshit you're experiencing online, take a break. Information overload is a real thing.

Spotting bullshit online isn't always easy, but you can create a mindset of skepticism to keep you safe. Fact-checking is always something you can do. Double-check any source you find and check the website's track record. Being mindful that people don't always tell the exact truth online helps you avoid accidentally spreading misinformation yourself.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Do you ever feel exhausted by the amount of information flying at you every day? It's normal if you do. We're bombarded by stories, numbers, opinions, and statistics daily, and it’s hard to understand what is true and what is nothing but lies. It doesn't help when people use underhanded tactics to pack a lie into something that could be true.

Indeed, there's a lot of truth online, but there's also a massive amount of misinformation, and separating fact from fiction isn't easy. So, learning to fact-check and think outside the box will protect your mind and sanity. But being skeptical doesn't mean always distrusting and looking for alternative reasons or answers to everything. It means knowing that there is a lot of bullshit out there. It's a state of awareness that can protect you whenever the need arises.

We tend to simply shake our heads at conspiracy theories and misinformation spreading around online. But we fail to understand just how serious it can be because everyone thinks differently. On the one hand, it might be so 'out there’ that you're sure nobody will believe it. On the other hand, that story may match someone’s confirmation bias, and they believe it to be true. So, they share it with someone else, who starts to question whether it might be the case or not. And before you know it, that 'out there' theory has gone viral. What if that theory is something dangerous or damaging?

While the Internet has offered us many positive things, it's also given us a significant misinformation headache. But, it's not just the online world where bullshit reigns supreme. Anything can be packaged up with a pretty bow on it so that it looks too good to be true. If that's the case, it usually is.

Try this

• Cut down on how much social media you use in order to avoid overloading yourself with information.
• If you're unsure of something, fact-check it by searching the website and looking at other theories.
• Don't share anything on social media that you feel may not be accurate or that you're simply not sure of.

Read it till the end?
